teacher_notes.beginner: Focus on even stick heights. Match the sound of each hand.
teacher_notes.intermediate: Start using fingers and wrist together. Stay relaxed at the fulcrum.
teacher_notes.advanced: Push for Moeller-assisted speed. If you tense up, drop 10 BPM.

name: Single Stroke Four
description: Four alternating strokes followed by a stop: RLRL

teacher_notes.beginner: Accent the first stroke of each group clearly. Let the unaccented notes be softer.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The contrast between accented and unaccented strokes is the whole point. Exaggerate it.
teacher_notes.advanced: Try leading with your weak hand (LRLR) to develop balance.

teacher_notes.beginner: Practice each double as a controlled bounce. The second stroke comes from rebound, not a new arm motion.
teacher_notes.intermediate: At medium tempos, use wrist for first stroke and finger/wrist rebound for second.
teacher_notes.advanced: At high tempos this becomes a pressure roll. Relax your grip slightly and let the stick bounce freely.

name: Single Paradiddle
description: RLRR LRLL — the most fundamental compound rudiment

teacher_notes.beginner: Say 'pa-ra-did-dle' out loud while playing. Each syllable is one stroke.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The accent on beat 1 of each group should pop. Everything else is quieter.
teacher_notes.advanced: Orchestrate around the kit — accent on snare, other notes on toms or different surfaces.

name: Flam
description: A grace note immediately before the primary stroke

teacher_notes.beginner: The grace note should be very quiet and very close to the primary stroke — almost simultaneous but not quite.
teacher_notes.intermediate: Keep your grace note hand low (1–2 inches) and primary stroke hand high (8–10 inches).
teacher_notes.advanced: Flamacues, flam accents, and flam taps all derive from this. Own it at all tempos.

name: Flam Accent
description: Flam followed by two taps: lRlr rLrl

teacher_notes.beginner: This lives in triplet feel. Count '1-trip-let 2-trip-let' while playing.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The primary flam stroke on 1 and 2 should be clearly louder. Taps should be controlled, not sloppy.
teacher_notes.advanced: A staple in rudimental drumming. Apply it in a musical context — fills, jazz brushwork.

name: Five Stroke Roll
description: RRLLR LLRRL — five strokes ending on a single

teacher_notes.beginner: Think of it as two doubles plus an accent: RR LL R. The R on the end is the 'point' of the roll.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The accent at the end of each five-stroke group should arrive exactly on the beat.
teacher_notes.advanced: Essential for fills. Practice moving the accent around the kit.

name: Nine Stroke Roll
description: Four doubles plus a single: RRLLRRLLr

teacher_notes.beginner: Four doubles then a tap. Count every double stroke as one unit.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The final accent lands on the 'and' of beat 2 in 4/4. Be precise.
teacher_notes.advanced: Combine nine-stroke rolls with five-stroke rolls for longer fill phrases.

teacher_notes.beginner: This is a single paradiddle with an extra RL in front: RL + RLRR.
teacher_notes.intermediate: In 6/8 time, this loops perfectly. Try it in a triplet feel.
teacher_notes.advanced: Orchestrate around the kit. The two diddles create natural accent opportunities.

name: Drag
description: Two grace notes before a primary stroke: rrR llL

teacher_notes.beginner: Two quiet grace notes, then one loud primary stroke. The grace notes are a fast double.
teacher_notes.intermediate: At slow tempos, play two distinct quiet strokes. At faster tempos, they blur into a buzz.
teacher_notes.advanced: The drag is the foundation of the single drag tap, double drag tap, and drag paradiddle.
